cards.text: {{c1::Association}} is a relationship where the class {{c2::uses-an}} otherwise unrelated object.

cards.front: What is the key difference between association and composition/aggregation?
cards.back: In association, the associated object is otherwise unrelated to the class (not a part-of or whole/part relationship). It's simply an object the class uses.

cards.explanation:
To qualify as association, an object and an associated object must have the following relationship:
  • The associated object (member) is otherwise unrelated to the object (class)
  • The associated object can belong to more than one object at a time
  • The associated object does NOT have its existence managed by the object
  • The associated object may or may not know about the existence of the object

cards.text: Associations may be implemented via {{c1::pointer}} or {{c2::reference}}, or by more indirect means such as holding the {{c3::index}} or {{c4::key}} of the associated object.

cards.front: What are the possible directionalities of an association relationship?
cards.back: Association can be either unidirectional (one object knows about the other) or bidirectional (both objects know about each other).

cards.text: In an association, the relationship verb is {{c1::uses-a}}, and the relationship type is {{c2::otherwise unrelated}}.

cards.back: Association relationship verb - indicates the class uses an otherwise unrelated object

cards.question: What type of relationship is demonstrated here?
cards.explanation: The Driver uses-a Car (via its ID), but they are otherwise unrelated objects. The association is implemented indirectly by storing the Car's ID rather than a pointer or reference.

cards.back: A reflexive association (also called bidirectional association) is when both objects in the association know about each other. For example, a Doctor knows about their Patients, and each Patient knows about their Doctors.

cards.front: Can an associated object belong to multiple objects at the same time?
cards.back: Yes. In an association, the associated object can belong to more than one object at a time, similar to aggregation.

cards.front: What is the main difference between association and aggregation?
cards.back: In aggregation, the part is still conceptually part of the whole (whole/part relationship). In association, the objects are otherwise unrelated - one just uses the other without a part-of relationship.

cards.text: Unlike composition and aggregation where the part never knows about the whole, in association the associated object {{c1::may or may not}} know about the existence of the object using it.
Association can be {{c1::unidirectional}} (one object knows about the other) or {{c2::bidirectional}} (both objects know about each other).
Associations can be implemented directly via {{c1::pointer or reference}}, or indirectly by holding an {{c2::index or key}}.
